model.label: GPT-5.2
model.description: OpenAI • Smaller GPT frontier model tuned for fast, cost-efficient reasoning and tool use.

pick.rationale: Kansas has a large baseline quality edge in the efficiency data available (KenPom #21 / Torvik #22 with a top-10 caliber defense), and that defensive floor is usually what prevents 4/13 upsets from materializing. California Baptist’s path is real—CBU has a high-end scorer (D. Daniels Jr. at 23.2 PPG per ESPN) and can swing a single game with shooting variance—but Kansas’ athleticism and ability to string together stops should limit clean looks and create separation over 40 minutes. With no clear, verified availability red flags surfaced in the quick scan, I’m siding with the higher two-way profile and tournament-tested roster.

reasoningStep.title: East 4/13: Kansas vs California Baptist
reasoningStep.summary: Kansas advances due to a significant two-way efficiency advantage and a defense that should travel well in a one-game setting, while acknowledging CBU’s upset path via high-end scoring and shooting variance.
evidence: Kansas ratings edge: KenPom #21 (AdjO 118.4, AdjD 94.0) and Torvik #22 (AdjO 117.95, AdjD 94.38).
Market/model context from ESPN game page: Matchup Predictor KU 92.6% vs CBU 7.4%; odds snapshot shows Kansas about -14.5 and -1200 ML.
CBU upset path: ESPN lists D. Daniels Jr. at 23.2 PPG, giving the Lancers a credible shot-creation/variance route if he gets hot.
Availability check: no confirmed, material injury/availability news found in the quick scan that would flip the projection.

toolCalls.summary: Returned combined Torvik/KenPom ratings for 2 teams.

Lee and Alonzo Olmedo 2026 Tournament Challenge guide to team mascots, school colors and famous alumni 22h Sach Chandan Men's tournament: Best bets for the round of 64, plus Eliminator picks High Point Panthers 3h Mark Zinno Six tips on how to bet the men's tournament like a veteran 2d Mark Zinno Reactions to the 2026 men's NCAA tournament field of 68 Arizona Wildcats 2d Joe Lunardi Ament, Dybantsa, and co. - Nine African players to watch at March Madness Duke Blue Devils 11h Leonard Solms Men's basketball coaching carousel tracker: Who could be up next at Butler? Boston College Eagles 1d Jeff Borzello Joe Lunardi's ultimate guide to men's March Madness 2026 Illustration by ESPN Joe Lunardi Mar 17, 2026, 09:45 AM ET Close Resident college basketball bracketologist for ESPN Contributor to SportsCenter, ESPN Insider Published first public bracket in 1995 Follow on X Multiple Authors Email Print Open Extended Reactions Our men's NCAA tournament preview has evolved since the first was created 30-odd years ago. It's how Bracketology began, when a handful of hoop nerds gathered during "Selection Weekend" to put together an 80-page postseason "Blue Ribbon" guide. It was the ultimate conclusion to spending the college basketball season poring over résumés, watching games and evaluating strengths, weaknesses and possibilities. All on the crew's own time. The guide has shrunk a tad in length and is no longer in print, but it still fulfills the same role: outlining the crucial information you need to fill out brackets. We've made the case for exactly how each team will advance -- and how they won't. Bracketologist in Chief Joe Lunardi also ran the numbers one last time to forecast where each will actually finish in the final field of 68. The results might or might not surprise you -- and could help you fill out those brackets . modelTrace.content:
You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
reasoningStep must contain id, title, summary, evidence.

Boston College Eagles 1d Jeff Borzello Joe Lunardi's ultimate guide to men's March Madness 2026 Illustration by ESPN Joe Lunardi Mar 17, 2026, 09:45 AM ET Close Resident college basketball bracketologist for ESPN Contributor to SportsCenter, ESPN Insider Published first public bracket in 1995 Follow on X Multiple Authors Email Print Open Extended Reactions Our men's NCAA tournament preview has evolved since the first was created 30-odd years ago. It's how Bracketology began, when a handful of hoop nerds gathered during "Selection Weekend" to put together an 80-page postseason "Blue Ribbon" guide. It was the ultimate conclusion to spending the college basketball season poring over résumés, watching games and evaluating strengths, weaknesses and possibilities. All on the crew's own time. The guide has shrunk a tad in length and is no longer in print, but it still fulfills the same role: outlining the crucial information you need to fill out brackets. We've made the case for exactly how each team will advance -- and how they won't. Bracketologist in Chief Joe Lunardi also ran the numbers one last time to forecast where each will actually finish in the final field of 68. The results might or might not surprise you -- and could help you fill out those brackets .
